body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

core things we're focusing on in ippm it's metrics so that's like like what kind of stuff do we want to measure what what do we want to understand about our networks uh and then we're looking at methods like how do we actually what do we do to obtain these metrics pretty much and then finally we also have then the protocols like what what are they what are the actual interactions we need to do what are the bits on the wire and all of that um so these are the three core things that we're doing uh next slide please so we start looking at what we mean with metrics so here's a quote from from from an RFC that tries to Define uh what we mean with metrics so um we try to standardize metrics that can be applied to the Quality performance and reliability of internet data delivered services and applications running over transport layer protocols for instance TCP or UDP so that's really the scope of things and there's a lot of things you can measure and we're trying to sort of Define what these metrics mean and give them well-defined names so that we can use them and reason about them so things like one-way delay round trip delay packet delay variation people like to call it Jitter we call it packet delay variation connectivity one-way packet loss etc etc it's a lot of things and we try to have clear-cut definitions of these things so that we can reason about them and design protocols to actually measure them so so these this is a really core fundamental part of what we do in ippm next slide please so but of course you need some methods to to obtain these metrics so there are various types of methods that are being defined in ippm and we have like three broad broad categories of methods for obtaining these these uh different performance metrics so we have what we call Active methods and that's when you really inject traffic into the network using some protocol and you you you measure what happens with that traffic that you you are injecting injecting uh and then we have the passive passive measurements and that's when you observe packets that are not injected by by you as an observer you're more just looking at stuff flying over the wire and yeah deriving your metrics from that and of course we have hybrid methods that are that are combining these two ways so you might have some actor that's generating some adding some fields to your to the traffic and you have some measurement points that not that don't have to be directly related to or actively injecting this traffic but you might be tagging and marking packets on the path um and and we usually call this then hybrid methods and there are a bunch of uh um methods that have been defined so we have things like the alternate marking method which is where you you you set the single bit in in an IP header and then flip this bit in various ways to be able to measure things like packet loss we have things called in c2o and M where where where uh where you tag on telemarket data onto packets we have IP capacity methods Etc lots of mesh methods being defined in various ways and we'll go look at a few of these and what's going on with them so we can go to the next slide another important thing that ipbm has been doing like just going through the history here so uh is that we've been working on the protocols then and for active for active measurement uh there there's a protocol Suite that has been very important uh that has been defined by by ippm in this this omtwamp and stamp protocol so it's OMP is this one-way active measurement protocol so you're using this to be to to uh to measure sort of one-way properties of of your network it's an active protocol so you're you're injecting traffic into the network between between clients and servers and then we have two-way active protocol active measurement protocol that does the same but you're actually kind of reflecting the test data so you you can you can measure you can measure kind of uh two-way properties of of the of the traffic so if you remember we have these different metrics referring to one-way stuff and two-way stuff and then there's a simplified architecture around this uh t-wamp we call it stamps a simple two-way active measurement protocol so these this is really the the core fundamentals of what ippm has been doing um but now we can move on to kind of the where we are right now so current status next slide please um so at the moment we have uh 11 um working group documents so if you remember I said we had 15 negative documents so 11 of these are currently working working group documents and four of these are documents that have been submitted to the isg and they are in various stages of the of the process there um next slide please so we have active work going on on new metrics new methods and new protocols so kind of keeping into the charter there but of course there's also quite a lot of work on like maintenance of these protocols that we have been defining and also extension students to like make them more up-to-date for various reasons yeah next slide so what we can say right now uh looking at what's going on in ippm right now I would say that we have kind of two broad scopes of of work that is going on um which is somewhat intersecting but also somewhat kind of uh not uh so we have a lot of work going on uh about like being able to measure stuff as an operator so like measuring things within your domain so we usually call it a limited domain where you're typically or an operator and you're in control of your infrastructure so there's a lot of work in that space but we also have a lot of work um on on sort of methods and and protocols that are more designed for being able to kind of measure any network path between Two Hosts or endpoints so uh yeah test protocols that you can spin up as a client somewhere and test towards any arbitrary server and and then measure your results um so next slide so some examples of of these uh these categories of work that we have right now is we have this alternate Market marking method that was just recently uh published as a standards track RFC which is about tagging uh tagging packets with single bits to be able to do sort of passive monitoring of uh of these uh of these packets typically this is something you would do within some Network domain we have this thing called I ioam which we'll be talking a bit more about which is also really about doing measurements within a specific domain tagging your packets adding data to them Telemetry data various forms we're defining new metrics which are typically very much targeted to to to operators and like this we have something called Precision availability metrics which is about um like observing adherence to specific uh like service layer objectives and how how like how much do we adhere to these objectives and things like that so that's very typically focused on on kind of operator domains limited domains and such uh in in the other space we we have various work we have some extensions to this o and t-wamp protocols we have work on what we call IP capacity measurements so it's like some protocols on how you actively determine the IP capacity of some Network you can also use use that for deriving other metrics and we have some interesting work on which is called responsiveness under working conditions which is really not just measuring your network but really measuring from a user perspective so it's it's it's including a kind of entire web protocol stack so TCP HTTP 2 TLS all of that is part of of the measurements and you you're you're deriving interesting metrics you using a kind of more user-centric uh setup and I will be talking a bit about these um so next slide please so we'll start talking a little bit about this in C2 administ this ioam so it's it's short for in C2 operation Administration and maintenance so this is really on this left hand side we're looking at we're looking at obtaining Telemetry data specifically as an operator or an operator of some kind of limited domain so this is a set of documents that specifies then kind of performance monitoring and tracing uh in what we call limited domains and there is some formal definition of limited domains but generally it would be kind of a domain where some operator or some entities is in full control of of of of the of all the nodes in in that path um so what IIM does is that it records o m information within packets when the packets traverses a specific Network domain so all of these Transit nodes would would uh would look for iom headers and then they would read that and then they would calculate some data and then either append that data into these packets and forward them along this path or it could just store the data and then export them using other metrics other methods as well but it's really about having this in band o m packets that you that you either trigger you to measure stuff trigger to export stuff or yeah Etc um and you would have a set of nodes in this type of in this type of architecture so you would have what we call an iom encapsulating node that then that adds these iom headers to packets and then you have a bunch of Transit nodes that that that reads these packets um looks for okay what headers are here does that mean that I need to do something maybe I need to to measure something and maybe I need to add something to this packet before forwarding etc etc and then finally at the end of this domain we have a decapsulating node that would remove the iom headers um from these packets and then keep forwarding them and like I said this iom data can be added directly to to packets or exported a separate o m packets so there are multiple options for handling the the tracing pretty much and the Telemetry export um and it's important because this is designed to be completely independent independent from underlying uh encapsulating protocols so you can Implement o m on various layers and you can even have multiple layers of iom stacked on top of each other depending on how your protocol Suite looks like so yeah short overview of this next slide please so a little bit of the status of this iom work it's been quite active work in the ippm working group and currently we have three published rfcs around this specifying the data fields so what are the kind of data that we're interested in in these in these Transit nodes and such and then we have various ways of of uh of looking at the exporting of data we have some active documents a few of these are in submitted to the isg and or in various stages of completion we have an informational document called IAM deployment with talk which talks a little bit about these various deployment scenarios you can have for for setting up an iom work um we have I am conf state which is is a method for uh kind of discovering different iom capabilities within your domain um we have IEM IPv6 options so that's one realization of iom um some active working group document as well we have some Yang modeling and we have some data Integrity work so that you you can kind of protect your your iom options yeah next slide please so moving on to something completely different uh the working group like I said we had these two two different broad Scopes sort of the one scope is really focusing on on network Telemetry as an operator and this is sort of far on the other end which is this work uh which is called responsiveness on the working conditions so this is a measurement method and Metric targeting end users with the main goal would be to kind of highlight how much uh response how much your responsiveness perceived responsiveness as a user can be degraded due to due to buffer bloat so the idea is really to kind of uh load up the network path as much as possible uh using uh using standard protocols that that a user would normally use for a big part of its uh normal interactions with the internet so it would be using TCP TLS HTTP 2. and you generate enough traffic to achieve what it's described as working conditions and this is done by kind of gradually adding more and more load generating TCP connections uh so that kind of not just one individual not just one single TCP connection because you will likely be received window limited and you might not actually reach this point where you really kind of saturate a bottleneck buffer and such so you keep adding load to the network until you you decide that you have reached what's called working conditions and when you when you reach these working conditions you start measuring something called responsiveness um and this responsiveness is a metric defined in this document it's it's sort of defined in terms of round trips per minute um and it's you you measure this by sending kind of probe requests so you keep sending HTTP get requests while you're have while you're in this state of having loaded the network and then they're sending two types of probes some probes are sent on the load generating connections themselves and some probes are sent on separate connections and then all of this is combined apply some statistics and you calculate responsiveness and um yeah it's it's in the idea with this one is really to present something that is Meaningful to an end user so if the iom stuff that we were looking at that was really data targeted to to uh to operators this is more for an end user and they serve quite different purposes um but anyway this is a active working group document right now it's it's uh it's actively being worked on it's it's progressing nicely through through the working group I would say um so yeah next slide please and then we have a kind of similar uh active active method and protocol as well looking at similar stuff but from a slightly different angle so this is called IP capacity measurement so this is derived from some some work that was done on defining an IP capacity metric in a method and now this work being done on a protocol to to measure this so this instead of like in this responsiveness work we we had we were using a really a user-centric uh protocol stack so we have TCP HTTP 2 TLS all of these things talking to real HTTP servers this one is more focused on just measuring purely the network component of of yeah of your performance so it's using UDP for this so it's it's using UDP both for load generation and control measurement control messages so load is generated in One Direction between a client and a server and you have another endpoint that the other endpoint then sends so one endpoint sends load pdus and the other one sends frequent status messages so here here again similar to to the to the previous method is that you you load up your network Until you realize that it's it's is fully saturated uh and then you can report things like what is the current IP capacity but this also enables uh metrics similar to the responsiveness that was defined in the in the previous method so you can use this for for kind of defining or measuring things like working latency and such um but using two very different uh two very different ways of obtaining this um so this is also some interesting work that goes on um next slide and finally we are um of course doing quite a lot of protocol maintenance and extensions to previously uh standardized protocols so we have a lot of you know small extensions to to to this uh omtwamp stamp Etc so we have we're making sure that it works over like for instance link aggregation groups and things like this so you can measure really uh like different components of your path um extensions to make a stamp work with segment routing uh Yang modeling and then there's some work going on on defining version two of of uh PDM which is uh some IPv6 destination options for for for for measurement and this work is really going about protecting these these options so um encrypting them pretty much so making them more secure to use um so this is some of the main work we're doing and finally we can go to the next slide um so what's next as you see we have quite a lot of active work in the working group and there is quite a lot of work related to the working group that might end up in it so currently we have 16 related internet drafts and lots of potential work there's a lot of interest in sort of both of these areas that I talked about both this kind of endpoint user-centric type of measurements but also this kind of operator domain Telemetry work so this is this is an this is part of our agenda we had yesterday and so you see there's always a lot of interest in bringing new work to this group so we had so basically we never have time to give longer talks for for all the work that people want to bring in so we we always like every ippm session we've had now for the last three or four itfs we we always do this lightning talk way where we we allow people to bring one slide and talk about their work and then hopefully
